glossary.definition: A mathematical function used in logistic regression to squash the value of the linear function within the range of [0,1].
glossary.sentence: After receiving the linear function's value, it applies a sigmoid function to squash the value of teh linear function within the range of [0,1] using the sigmoid function. The threshold value of 0.5 at which the function crosses the y axis is used to decide about a class/category of a data object.

glossary.definition: A machine learning problem where the goal is to attribute a data object to a specific class or predict a discrete value, including binary and multiclass classification.
glossary.sentence: In a classification task, it is necessary to attribute a data object to a specific class/category or, in other words, to predict a discrete value. The simplest case of classification is the so-called binary classification when data are classified in two classes or, in other words, the output variable can take either of two possible values.

glossary.definition: A simple supervised learning algorithm that solves both classification and regression tasks by finding the most similar neighbour(s) in the existing dataset for a new data object and assigning its category/class label based on distance metrics.
glossary.sentence: K-nearest neighbour or kNN algorithm is a very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks. Let’s define the idea of this algorithm based on the classification task.

glossary.definition: The basis for the algorithm, determined based on feature-based descriptions of data objects (feature similarity). It differs for different data types. For continuous features, geometric distance is calculated; for categorical, Hamming distance.
glossary.sentence: The concept of similarity (sometimes called distance, proximity, or closeness) makes the basis for the algorithm. In this case, the similarity of data objects is determined based on feature-based descriptions of data objects (the so-called feature similarity) (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: A method to determine the similarity of data objects based on their features.
glossary.sentence: The notion of similarity differs for different data types (Kubat, 2017). For continuous feature values, it is possible to calculate the geometric distance between any pair of data objects: the closer to each other the data objects, the greater their mutual similarity.

glossary.definition: A measure of the 'straight line' or geometric distance between two points in space. In machine learning, it's used to calculate similarity between data objects with continuous feature values.
glossary.sentence: For each data object in the dataset, the algorithm will (Fig. 17): calculate the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset;

glossary.definition: A measure of the distance between two points in a grid-based system, calculated as the sum of the absolute differences of their Cartesian coordinates. In machine learning, it's used to calculate similarity between data objects with continuous feature values.
glossary.sentence: For each data object in the dataset, the algorithm will (Fig. 17): calculate the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset;

glossary.definition: A measure of the minimum number of substitutions required to change one string into another. In machine learning, it's used to calculate similarity between data objects with categorical feature values.
glossary.sentence: For each data object in the dataset, the algorithm will (Fig. 17): calculate the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset;

glossary.definition: A version of the k-nearest neighbors algorithm where a new data object is classified by finding a single, but closest data object in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: As quite usually datasets include mixed-type data – both continuous and categorical, to cope with these data, we can rely on the sum of the squared distances along corresponding features (Kubat, 2017): Figure 14 demonstrates the calculation of the distance for mixed-type feature values. The features “Average mark in the semester work” and “The number of missed lectures” have continuous values, therefore the Euclidean distance is calculated. In turn, the features that represent the student's evaluations in the laboratory works have categorical values, so the Hamming distance is used to determine whether the feature values of the data objects are the same or different.

glossary.definition: A machine learning algorithm that classifies a new data object based on the output values (labels) of its k nearest neighbours in the dataset. The distance between data objects is calculated using Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance. The output for classification tasks is the mode of the k labels and for regression tasks, it's the mean of the k labels.
glossary.sentence: It also does not have some assumptions about data distribution. On the other hand, the algorithm is an impractical choice with a large number of data objects because, in this case, the algorithm becomes computationally expensive and time-consuming as it calculates the distance between all the training data objects and a new one data object.

glossary.definition: A requirement for a distance metric that states the direct path between two points should not be longer than any other path.
glossary.sentence: Any distance metric must satisfy the following requirements (Kubat, 2017): 1. the distance must never be negative; 2. the distance between two identical feature vectors is zero; 3. the distance from x to y is the same as the distance from y to x ; 4. the metric must satisfy the triangular inequality: In general, we can differentiate two versions of the algorithm.

glossary.definition: An unsupervised machine learning algorithm that groups similar data objects into clusters based on their feature vectors. The algorithm minimizes the sum of distances between data objects and their assigned cluster centroids.
glossary.sentence: The K-Means algorithm is one of the popular algorithms of unsupervised machine learning. According to (Jones, 2009), “the algorithm is popular primarily because it works relatively well and is extremely simple both to understand and to implement” .

glossary.definition: The centre of a cluster in K-Means algorithm. It's the average of all feature vectors within that cluster.
glossary.sentence: A centroid is a centre of a cluster around which data objects are grouped based on their distance to the centroid. It is the average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: A subset of similar data objects in an unsupervised learning context. Each cluster is characterized by similarity among its members and difference from other clusters.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, each cluster is made of one or more data objects and characterised by two aspects: the similarity of data objects within the cluster; the difference of data objects between the clusters.

glossary.definition: The number of clusters, K, that need to be generated in K-Means algorithm. It's selected by a trial-and-error approach and is a critical factor influencing the clustering result.
glossary.sentence: Specify the number of clusters, K , that need to be generated by this algorithm. Thus, K is the hyperparameter of this algorithm.

glossary.definition: A metric used in machine learning algorithms like kNN and K-Means to quantify similarity or dissimilarity between data objects.
glossary.sentence: The concept of distance was considered in detail in the second topic of the module in regard to the kNN algorithm. It is also used in unsupervised machine learning.

glossary.definition: A method for selecting hyperparameters, like K in K-Means, where different values are tried and evaluated until satisfactory results are achieved.
glossary.sentence: Usually, it is selected by a trial-and-error approach.

glossary.definition: An algorithm in which clusters are successively merged or split based on their similarity, resulting in a hierarchy of clusters (Hastie, 2017).
glossary.sentence: This algorithm does not demand to specify the number of clusters before the algorithm operation.

glossary.definition: A structure in hierarchical clustering where each level has clusters formed by merging/dividing clusters at the next lower level, with a single data object forming a cluster at the lowest level and all data objects in one cluster at the highest level.
glossary.sentence: Hierarchical clustering is about building a hierarchy of clusters that is characterised by the following aspects (Hastie, 2017) : the clusters at each level of the hierarchy are created by merging/dividing clusters at the next lower level; at the lowest level, each cluster contains a single data object; at the highest level, there is only one cluster containing all of the data objects.

glossary.definition: A bottom-up approach in hierarchical clustering where each data object initially belongs to its own cluster and pairs are recursively merged into a single cluster based on the smallest intergroup dissimilarity until one cluster with all data objects is formed.
glossary.sentence: Agglomerative hierarchical clustering is a bottom-up approach to building a hierarchy of clusters. Therefore, each data object initially is attributed to its own cluster. Then, at each level, a selected pair of clusters is recursively merged into a single cluster.

glossary.definition: A top-down approach in hierarchical clustering where one existing cluster is recursively split into two new clusters based on the largest between-group dissimilarity until each data object forms its own cluster.
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach. Therefore, it starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it. Then, at each hierarchy level, it recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters.

glossary.definition: Methods used to measure similarity (distance) between clusters for merging purposes in hierarchical clustering. Examples include Complete-linkage, Single-linkage, and Average-linkage.
glossary.sentence: Several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods . The most used methods are (Hastie, 2017) : Complete-linkage ; Single-linkage ; Average-linkage.

glossary.definition: A tree-like diagram used to illustrate the hierarchy of clusters in hierarchical clustering (Hastie, 2017).
glossary.sentence: (as seen in Figure 6) Cut-off 1 intersects two vertical lines, and we receive two clusters... Cut-off 2 intersects 3 vertical lines, so we receive three clusters.

glossary.definition: A point in hierarchical clustering where the dendrogram is cut to determine the final number of clusters (as seen in Figure 6).
glossary.sentence: Cut-off 1 intersects two vertical lines, and we receive two clusters... Cut-off 2 intersects 3 vertical lines, so we receive three clusters.

glossary.definition: Metrics used to evaluate the performance of a machine learning algorithm, comparing different algorithms on the same dataset or analyzing the behavior of one algorithm with varying hyperparameters (Pai, 2021).
glossary.sentence: Metrics for evaluating the machine learning model performance are used to: compare the performance of the different algorithms used on the same dataset and/or analyse the behaviour of the same algorithm by tuning values of its hyperparameters.

glossary.definition: A metric for regression tasks that measures the average squared prediction errors for individual data objects. It provides positive non-zero values and is calculated by averaging the square of differences between predicted and actual output values.
glossary.sentence: The Mean squared error (MSE) measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects.

glossary.definition: A metric derived from MSE by applying a square root, providing an error measure in the same unit as the target variable. It is calculated by taking the square root of the average of squared differences between predicted and actual output values.
glossary.sentence: Root mean square error (RMSE) applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.

glossary.definition: A metric for regression tasks that measures the average of absolute differences between predicted output values and actual output values. It provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.
glossary.sentence: Mean absolute error (MAE) is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values.

glossary.definition: A coefficient indicating how close a model fits the data. It ranges from 0 to 1, where 0 indicates no fit and 1 indicates perfect fit. It represents the proportion of variance in the dependent variable that is predictable from the independent variables (Kubat, 2017).
glossary.sentence: R2 coefficient is also known as the coefficient of determination.

glossary.definition: A type of classification task where there are only two possible outcomes or classes.
glossary.sentence: The structure of the matrix differs a little bit for the binary classification task and multiclass classification. Table 1 represents the structure of the confusion matrix for the binary classification...

glossary.definition: In binary classification, a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1).
glossary.sentence: Correct predictions: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1).

glossary.definition: In binary classification, a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0).
glossary.sentence: True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0).

glossary.definition: In binary classification, a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1).
glossary.sentence: Erroneous predictions: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1).

glossary.definition: In binary classification, a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0).
glossary.sentence: Erroneous predictions: False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0).

glossary.definition: A two-dimensional table of counts that shows how often each class/category is correctly or incorrectly classified as another category (Kubat, 2017; Russell & Norvig. 2010). It represents detailed information on the number of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category.
glossary.sentence: Thus, it represents detailed information on the number of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category. Each row in a confusion matrix represents an actual class, while each column represents a predicted class (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: A type of classification task where there are three or more classes.
glossary.sentence: In multiclass classification, the main diagonal of the confusion matrix represents the number of data objects correctly predicted (true positives for a specific class/category). Still, other cells correspond to the number of data objects mislabelled by the algorithm.

glossary.definition: A case where the model correctly predicts a positive class or category (Geron, 2017).
glossary.sentence: The mentioned outcomes are typically summarised in a confusion matrix (Kubat, 2017) . It is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category.

glossary.definition: An incorrect prediction that a data object belongs to a positive class or category (Geron, 2017).
glossary.sentence: In the statement “false positive”, "false" means incorrect prediction, and "positive" that a data object is classified as positive.

glossary.definition: A case where the model correctly predicts a negative class or category (Geron, 2017).
glossary.sentence: Other cells that do not intersect the column and row corresponding to a specific class/category represent true negatives for this specific class.

glossary.definition: An incorrect prediction that a data object does not belong to a positive class or category (Geron, 2017).
glossary.sentence: Taking a specific class/category, we can consider the values at the intersections of the column of the corresponding class with other classes as false positives, while values at the intersections of the row of the corresponding class with other classes as false negatives.

glossary.definition: A metric that evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects. It is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset (Kubat, 2017; Geron, 2017).
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects. It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017) : Accuracy for the multiclass classification is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset.

glossary.definition: A metric that evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives (Geron, 2017). It is calculated as the ratio of true positives to all positive predictions made by the classifier.
glossary.sentence: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. In other words, the accuracy of the positive predictions (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: Also known as sensitivity and true positive rate, it evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects (Kubat, 2017; Geron, 2017). It is calculated as the ratio of true positives to all actual positive data objects.
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects. According to (Kubat, 2017), recall is the probability that a positive data object will be correctly recognised by the algorithm.

glossary.definition: A dataset where classes are not evenly represented. For instance, in a binary classification task, one class might have significantly more instances than the other.
glossary.sentence: However, this metrics is not a clear indicator of the performance, especially if classes are imbalanced (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: A concept in machine learning classification tasks where optimizing for one (precision or recall) may lead to a decrease in the other.
glossary.sentence: This is called the precision/recall tradeoff .

glossary.definition: A metric used in classification tasks representing the harmonic mean of precision and recall. It estimates the number of data objects classified correctly.
glossary.sentence: The F1 score is one more metric used in the classification tasks. It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high (Geron, 2017) . Thus, it estimates the number of data objects classified correctly.

glossary.definition: A type of average, calculated as the reciprocal of the arithmetic mean of the reciprocals.
glossary.sentence: It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high (Geron, 2017) .

glossary.definition: In multiclass classification tasks, it's calculated by taking the macro average of precision and recall.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification task, macro F1 can be calculated by the macro average precision and recall.

glossary.definition: A graphical plot used to visualize classifier behaviour under different parameter values. It plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis).
glossary.sentence: The behaviour of the classifier under different values of its parameters can be visualised by the so-called ROC (The receiver operating characteristic) curve (Kubat, 2017) . The ROC curve plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter.

glossary.definition: Area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve. It provides a single-number summary of classifier performance, measuring the entire two-dimensional area underneath the ROC curve.
glossary.sentence: Area under the ROC Curve (AUC) allows comparing different classifiers. It provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve (Russell & Norvig, 2010) .

glossary.definition: A metric for evaluating clustering tasks. It represents the closeness of each data object in a cluster to data objects in neighbouring clusters.
glossary.sentence: One of the metrics that do not demand ground-truth labels is the Silhouette Score . The Silhouette Score is calculated by the following way: where a - average intra-cluster distance (the average distance between each point within a cluster), b - average inter-cluster distance (the average distance between all clusters).

glossary.definition: A cell in the human brain responsible for transmitting signals through axons to dendrites of other neurons.
glossary.sentence: The human brain consists of cells called neurons. Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig. Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons.

glossary.definition: An entity that has (1) one or more inputs represented by numerical values, (2) a set of real-valued weights for each input expressing its strength or importance, (3) an output represented by a numerical value which can be either a final solution or an input to other neurons. It combines the weighted inputs using a specific function (often summation), applies an activation function to determine the output signal, and sends it to the environment or other neurons.
glossary.sentence: In general, an artificial neuron has (1) one or more inputs (sometimes also called input signals) which are represented by numerical values that enter from the environment or are delivered by other neurons. The numerical values from the environment typically correspond to the values of data object features in the dataset. The inputs typically are labelled by indexed x; (2) a set of real-valued weights (one for each input) that express the strength, or, in other words, the importance of each neuron input in terms of its influence on the output. Weights are the basic means of long-term memory in artificial neural networks. They are determined in the process of neural network learning. Weights are typically represented by indexed w; (3) an output that is represented by a numerical value and can be either a final solution of the artificial neural network or output to other neurons. It is usually represented by indexed y.

glossary.definition: A type of synapse that promotes firing in a neuron.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: A type of synapse that inhibits firing in a neuron.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: The sum of all signals received at the neuron's synapses, calculated by multiplying incoming signal strengths by corresponding synaptic weights and adding them together. It represents the aggregate influence on the neuron.
glossary.sentence: If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight and an inhibitory synapse a negative weight, then we can calculate the strength of each synapse (by multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight). Adding all the synapses together gives the total strength of the signals received at the synapses – and hence the total input to the neuron.

glossary.definition: A level in a neuron where if the total input exceeds it, the neuron will fire.
glossary.sentence: If this total input exceeds the threshold of the neuron, then the neuron will fire (the fragment is adopted from (Bermúdez, 2014)).

glossary.definition: A layer of neurons in an artificial neural network between input and output layers. They do not directly interact with the external environment; instead, they process information received from the previous layer.
glossary.sentence: The layers of neurons between input and output layers are called hidden layers .

glossary.definition: A method used in artificial neural networks to combine input signals.
glossary.sentence: a function for combining input signals (for example, the summation function )

glossary.definition: A function that determines the output signal of a neuron, allowing artificial neural networks to model complex non-linear relationships.
glossary.sentence: The transfer (activation) function determines the output of the neuron. According to ( Negnevitsky , 2011), many activation functions have been tested, but only a few have found practical applications; the four common choices are the step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions.

glossary.definition: An additional input to an artificial neuron that provides offset from the origin in the line/hyperplane, allowing more flexibility in fitting the model. It is typically set to one and its weight is tuned by the learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A bias input which is also commonly applied, and it provides the offset of the line/hyperplane from the origin. If no bias existed, the line (or the hyperplane) would be restricted to pass through the origin, and the weights would only control the angle of the discriminant. The bias is commonly set to one, but the learning algorithm of an artificial neural network should tune its weight.

glossary.definition: A method used in artificial neural networks to calculate output signals from combined inputs.
glossary.sentence: a function for calculating an output signal (the activation function )

glossary.definition: A method used in adjusting the weights of artificial neural networks to improve performance based on the difference between desired and actual outputs.
glossary.sentence: a learning rule to adapt the weights

glossary.definition: A type of artificial neural network where connections are unidirectional, from input to output, without loops.
glossary.sentence: A feed-forward network has connections only in one direction . Every processing unit receives input from 'upstream' processing units and delivers output to 'downstream' processing units; there are no loops.

glossary.definition: A type of artificial neural network where outputs are fed back into inputs, forming a dynamic system that may exhibit various behaviors including stable states, oscillations, or chaos.
glossary.sentence: A recurrent network feeds its outputs back into its inputs. It means that the activation levels of the network form a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations or even chaotic behaviour.

glossary.definition: A type of processing unit in an artificial neural network that processes input based on summation and activation functions. It adjusts weights if the predicted output differs from the real output; otherwise, weight adjustments are not made.
glossary.sentence: a training example with its real classification label (the real output) is given to the perceptron; the perceptron processes it based on the summation and activation functions available in the processing unit; after receiving the output of the perceptron, the system checks if there is a difference between the predicted output and the real output; if the predicted output is different from the real output, then the perceptron adjusts the weights; otherwise, if the predicted output is the same as the actual output, then the adjustments of the weights are not made because the performance of the perceptron network is considered to be satisfactory;

glossary.definition: The simplest type of artificial neural network with practical applicability limited to learning linearly separable functions.
glossary.sentence: A Single Layer Perceptron is the simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions.

glossary.definition: The ability of artificial neural networks to maintain functionality even with partially damaged processing units or connections, due to the distributed nature of knowledge in the network.
glossary.sentence: At the same time, artificial neural networks are resistant to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units and network operation changes very little. It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation, and it is explained by the fact that the knowledge of the network is not stored in one place, but it is distributed all over the network ( Borisovs et al., 1998).

glossary.definition: The task of identifying the structure or pattern in data.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data;

glossary.definition: The task of finding the 'best' organization of constraints.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data; prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects; optimization or, in other words, finding the 'best' organization of constraints;

glossary.definition: The task of separating signal from background, factoring out irrelevant components of a signal.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data; prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects; optimization or, in other words, finding the 'best' organization of constraints; noise filtering, or separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal;

glossary.definition: The task of determining values of variables that cannot be measured easily but are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs; pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data; prediction, such as identifying a disease from symptoms, causes from effects; optimization or, in other words, finding the 'best' organization of constraints; noise filtering, or separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal; non-linear estimation determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables and others.

glossary.definition: A method used for computing weight corrections in artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: The weight correction is usually computed by applying the delta rule :

glossary.definition: One cycle where an artificial neural network uses all data objects in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes, representing the number of times the network works through the entire training dataset.
glossary.sentence: An epoch represents one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes. Therefore, the number of epochs corresponds to the number of times that the network will work through the entire training dataset.

glossary.definition: A type of artificial neural network with multiple layers between input and output layers.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the learning in the multilayer perceptron is based on the backpropagation algorithm .

glossary.definition: A neural network where information moves in only one direction—from input nodes, through hidden nodes (if any), to output nodes. The connections between nodes do not form a cycle.
glossary.sentence: It has ( Negnevitsky , 2011): an input layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers. It does not include computing neurons; an output layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons; at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features.

glossary.definition: A method used in artificial neural networks for calculating the gradient that is needed in the calculation of the weights updates, as part of supervised learning.
glossary.sentence: performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer;

glossary.definition: An algorithm used in multilayer perceptron for learning. It includes two main phases: feed-forwarding and backpropagation. During feed-forwarding, inputs are propagated from the input layer to the output layer. Backpropagation involves adjusting weights in all network layers through error propagation from the output layer to the input layer.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the learning in the multilayer perceptron is based on the backpropagation algorithm . It includes two main phases: feed-forwarding and backpropagation (Fig. The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding . In a supervised machine learning task, the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels.

glossary.definition: A phase of the backpropagation algorithm where inputs are propagated from the input layer to the output layer of a multilayer perceptron network.
glossary.sentence: During feed-forwarding, inputs are propagated from the input layer to the output layer .

glossary.definition: Processing units in the hidden layers of a neural network that contribute to some fraction of the total error in each of the output units.
glossary.sentence: The training data do not say what value the hidden processing units should have, and each hidden processing unit is responsible for some fraction of the error in each of the output units (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: A natural way of representing knowledge as used by human experts who often explain problem-solving using 'if... then...' constructions.
glossary.sentence: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...” (Negnevitsky , 2011)

glossary.definition: An intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine.
glossary.sentence: A knowledge-based intelligent system is an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: A repository containing a considerable number of production rules for real-world problems.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base for real-world problems includes a considerable number of production rules. In each iteration, all production rules stored in the knowledge base are matched against the pattern.

glossary.definition: The processing mechanism for knowledge in rule-based expert systems.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine. The advantages of this separation include ease of modifying the knowledge base without requiring a change in the code for program control and, conversely, the ability to alter the code for program control without changing the set of production rules (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 200; Negnevitsky , 2011)

glossary.definition: A structure that stores unrelated facts, such as purchase transactions or grades of students.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base differs from a database. The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students.

glossary.definition: A set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge in a computational structure like the knowledge base. It can be logical, networked, structured, or procedural/rule-based.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge. Using them, a knowledge representation or description is created. Several categories of knowledge representation schemas exist in the field of artificial intelligence (Grimm et al., 2007; Mylopoulos and Levesque, 1983): logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge; networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them.

glossary.definition: A type of artificial intelligence system that emulates the decision-making abilities of a human expert.
glossary.sentence: Thus, an expert system should explain its reasoning and justify the output ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A specialist who knows methods and tools of artificial intelligence and how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base, often working closely with domain experts.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base. However, the knowledge engineer is not a specialist in all problem domains. This is a reason why he/she works in a close collaboration with the domain expert.

glossary.definition: An individual who has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain, often possessing domain-specific heuristic knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, the domain expert has domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows him to solve problems in a more effective way. The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: The methods used for populating a knowledge base with problem-specific knowledge, which can be done through domain experts, automated systems, or machine learning.
glossary.sentence: How does knowledge get to the knowledge base? There are several main methods used for this purpose (Hopgood, 2001): 1) knowledge is acquired from a domain expert, processed by a specialist called a knowledge engineer and then added to the knowledge base, 2) the domain expert can describe and store knowledge using an automated knowledge acquisition system, and 3) nowadays intelligent computer systems are able to learn from examples.

glossary.definition: Psychological theories that form the basis of networked knowledge representation schemas, representing knowledge in a directed labelled graph.
glossary.sentence: Networked knowledge representation schemas are based on associationist theories of human mind that were developed in psychology.

glossary.definition: Relationships between different concepts in the human mind that define their meaning (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: These theories postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts (Luger, 2009), for example, the concept “university” for the most part of students could be associated with such concepts as lectures, students, professors, exams, science etc.

glossary.definition: The organization of knowledge where certain concepts are broader and subsume narrower ones (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: Research works in psychology have revealed that concepts are not only related by associations but they are also organized in a hierarchical manner.

glossary.definition: A knowledge representation schema where knowledge is represented as a graph with labelled nodes (concepts) and directed labelled arcs (relations between them). It includes a network of concepts and their relations (Luger, 2009; Callan, 2003; Mallach, 1994; Winston, 1992).
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: A relation in semantic networks represented by 'is a' title, showing hierarchical relations where narrower concepts are related to broader ones (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: A relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “subkind -kind”) is represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”). It allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: A relation in semantic networks represented by 'is an instance/example of' title, showing hierarchical relations where one specific object is related to a broader concept it belongs to (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”. It also allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to (Fig.7).

glossary.definition: A relation in semantic networks represented by 'Part of' title, showing relations between parts and whole objects where without the defined parts, the object will lose some important properties or its original appearance (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: A relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of “Part of” (Fig.8). It allows relating parts of the object to the whole object.

glossary.definition: A relation in semantic networks represented by 'property/has a property of' title, showing relations between objects/concepts and their properties (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of” (Fig.9). It allows relating objects/concepts to their properties.

glossary.definition: A relation in semantic networks represented by 'value' title, showing relations between properties and their values where this relation can come only after the relation 'property/has a property of' (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value”. It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values (Fig.10).

glossary.definition: An essential feature of frame-based expert systems. The generalization relation ensures inheritance in frames. Therefore, sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame.
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is an essential feature of frame-based expert systems ( Negnevitsky , 2011). The generalization relation ensures inheritance in frames. Therefore, sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame (Figure 11).

glossary.definition: A type of knowledge representation where nodes represent concepts, objects or facts; edges represent relationships between these entities. They are used to visually depict complex systems of interrelated concepts.
glossary.sentence: At the same time, semantic networks have disadvantages: they represent only declarative knowledge (knowledge about facts, objects, and concepts ); they do not have well-defined formal semantics and can introduce ambiguity both during the knowledge representation when the same unit of knowledge can be represented in different ways, and during the interpretation of a semantic network, when the content represented can be interpreted in different ways; they do not allow to represent specific types of knowledge like negation, disjunction, or also all/part/several/nobody/many, etc.

glossary.definition: A method of representing knowledge where each node in the graph represents a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts. Relations are represented using referencing principles, where one node references another by name.
glossary.sentence: Topic 5.2 - Structured Knowledge Representation Schemas Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge. However, comparing to the networked knowledge representation schemas, each node in the graph represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts.

glossary.definition: A data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation. It is a data structure that represents typical knowledge about an object/concept, or information relevant to stereotyped entities.
glossary.sentence: They are based on the following idea: “When one encounters a new situation (or makes a substantial change in one's view of the present problem) one selects from memory a structure called a Frame. This is a remembered framework to be adapted to fit reality by changing details as necessary.”

glossary.definition: Properties of objects/concepts in frames.
glossary.sentence: Each frame has a unique title or identifier in the knowledge base. Slots are properties of objects/concepts (Jones, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: An extension to the slot-value structure of frames. They represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value.
glossary.sentence: Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A frame that contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts. Used to represent a problem in an abstract form.
glossary.sentence: According to Minsky (1975), “collections of related frames are linked together into frame-systems” . Thus, to create a knowledge base for the frame-based expert system, we need to define and interrelate frames. There are two main types of frames (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): A class/generic frame.

glossary.definition: Includes a title of the class/set/type/kind, a set of common properties (slots), and values of properties that are common to all objects of the class/set/type/kind. Traditionally slot values in the class frame are empty or contain default values.
glossary.sentence: Typically, a class frame includes a title of the class/set/type/kind, a set of common properties, and values of properties that are common to all objects of the class/set/type/kind (Figure 4).

glossary.definition: A class derived from another class. Sub-class frames can contain new slots that characterize objects belonging to the sub-class.
glossary.sentence: Using this type of frames, we can also create sub-classes that are classes derived from other classes (Figure 5). The sub-class frames can contain new slots that are not included in the class frames but characterize objects belonging to the sub-class.

glossary.definition: A type of frame that represents an individual object or instance. Examples include TreeAlex (Person), BrownLucie (Person), Tom (Cat), Rex (Dog).
glossary.sentence: Finally, there are four instance frames: TreeAlex and BrownLucie are instances for the Person class frame; Tom is an instance for the Cat class frame; Rex is an instance for the Dog class frame.

glossary.definition: A principle used to relate two frames by using a unique title of one frame as a slot value in another frame.
glossary.sentence: A referencing principle is used to relate two frames. This means that a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: Relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame. Allows making hierarchies of frames where the most general frame is located at the highest level and the most specific ones – at the lower levels.
glossary.sentence: The Generalization relation . It relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame. It allows making hierarchies of frames where the most general frame is located at the highest level and the most specific ones – at the lower levels.

glossary.definition: Allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts by associating frames corresponding to the components of an object with a frame representing the object as a whole.
glossary.sentence: The Aggregation relation . It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9). In other words, frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole .

glossary.definition: Allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle.
glossary.sentence: Association or semantic relation . It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).

glossary.definition: A slot can have a procedural attachment which includes regular procedures and/or demons.
glossary.sentence: A slot can have a procedural attachment ( Negnevitsky , 2011). In general, it is possible to add regular procedures and/or demons.

glossary.definition: A self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system. Used for different purposes like generating an image of the object, calculating numerical values, acquiring data from external sources, etc.
glossary.sentence: A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system ( Negnevitsky , 2011). We can use such procedures for different purposes like generating an image of the object, calculating some numerical values, such as the amount of taxes to be paid, acquiring data from external sources, etc.

glossary.definition: A procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base. Implemented actions for slot-level manipulations. Defined in class and sub-class frames and inherited/executed in instance frames.
glossary.sentence: Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system (Jones, 2009; Luger,2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992). A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). Demons are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed (Mallach, 1994). Thus, demons control actions for slot-level manipulations (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: Triggered when the slot value is unspecified and it is necessary to calculate/to set it.
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it; IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes; IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame; IF-REMOVED – it is triggered when a slot value is deleted.

glossary.definition: Acquires knowledge about some object/concept by finding a corresponding frame and checking its slots. If necessary knowledge isn't found, applies inheritance and checks inherited demons IF-NEEDED. Explores local relations with other frames to get knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Although the demons are triggered automatically, their executable part (the code describing what will happen when the demon is called) should be developed by the developer of the intelligent computer system. Figure 12 shows an example of the use of demons in frames. Two demons are defined for the Client insurance card class frame: The demon IF-ADDED will calculate the value of the slot Ensured services when a new client (a frame instance) would be added to the knowledge base. The frame Card#2967 contains the calculated value for the slot Ensured services “Luggage insurance for free ” ; The demon IF-NEEDED will calculate the amount of discount for a particular client when the information on the discount will be requested. The inference engine of frames is based on the following principles: to acquire knowledge about some object/concept, find a corresponding frame and check its slots. If necessary knowledge is available in the object frame, return it; if necessary knowledge is not found, then the inference engine can apply the steps specified below and stop when the necessary knowledge is found: perform inheritance (if it is possible ); check inherited demons IF-NEEDED. If they are, trigger them and output the results; explore the local relations with other frames and try to get knowledge from them.

glossary.definition: A component of frame systems used to represent the generalization relation between frames.
glossary.sentence: The generalization relation is defined between TreeAlex-Person , BrownLucie-Person , Tom-Cat , Rex-Dog , Cat-Animal , Dog-Animal . It is represented as a Class slot in the sub-class frames and instance frames.

glossary.definition: A knowledge representation structure that stores information about an object or concept in a structured way, allowing for hierarchical organization of knowledge and inheritance. Frames can have executable demons to control access to slot values and ensure the knowledge base's integrity.
glossary.sentence: Frames store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way; frames provide the hierarchical organization of knowledge; frames allow the representation of various knowledge about objects; frames support inheritance; frames allow adding executable demons to slots to control access to slot values and, therefore, ensure the knowledge base's integrity.

glossary.definition: A chunk of knowledge about solving a problem, consisting of a condition (IF) and a consequence (THEN), joined by logic operators AND, OR, or both. It may include objects and their values linked by operators such as is, are, is not, are not, mathematical operators.
glossary.sentence: Each production rule is a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011). A production rule is presented in the following way (Callan, 2003; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011): IF condition THEN consequence - action/statement/conclusion

glossary.definition: A rule-based expert system consisting of a knowledge base (set of production rules), working memory, and inference engine. It uses problem-solving instructions rather than representing knowledge about objects/concepts.
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2005; Negnevitsky , 2011): a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine.

glossary.definition: A component that contains a pattern corresponding to the current state of the problem-solving process during the control cycle 'Recognize-act'.
glossary.sentence: While implementing the pattern-directed search, the control cycle performs the following steps: the working memory contains a pattern corresponding to the current state of the problem-solving process; the pattern is matched against the IF or THEN part (it depends on the search direction) of all rules available in the knowledge base;

glossary.definition: A description of the current state of the problem-solving process stored in the working memory.
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern .

glossary.definition: An intelligent system where domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules. It includes components like a knowledge base, working memory, inference engine, and user interface.
glossary.sentence: A rule-based expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: A fundamental mechanism in inference systems that implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The control cycle “Recognize-act” makes the basis of the inference mechanism. It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.

glossary.definition: A form of rules that is critical in controlling search and making the system behave logically. The structure and order of these rules determine how to search the knowledge base and how quickly it will be searched.
glossary.sentence: (the text fragment is based on the information given in (Luger, 2009))

glossary.definition: The set of enabled rules from which one rule is chosen to fire in conflict resolution.
glossary.sentence: (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011)

glossary.definition: The method used in choosing a rule to fire from those that are enabled.
glossary.sentence: (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011)

glossary.definition: A search strategy in rule-based expert systems where the process begins with a problem description or given data about the problem.
glossary.sentence: In data-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): the search begins with a problem description or given data about the problem (a pattern) that are added to the working memory;

glossary.definition: A search strategy in rule-based expert systems where an expert system has a goal and attempts to find evidence to prove it.
glossary.sentence: In backward chaining , an expert system has the goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.
glossary.sentence: (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011)

glossary.definition: Methods used to make the search process more effective: control through selection of search direction; control through selection of rule sequence and structure; control through selection of conflict resolution strategy.
glossary.sentence: Three search control strategies are used for this purpose (Luger, 2009): control through the selection of search direction; control through the selection of rule sequence and structure; control through the selection of conflict resolution strategy.

glossary.definition: An operator that introduces ambiguity when used in production rules.
glossary.sentence: try avoiding the use of OR in production rules because this operator introduces ambiguity; if it is not possible to avoid using OR, try avoiding the use of combinations of AND and OR in the same rule (Luger, 2009)

glossary.definition: An operator used in conjunction with the OR operator, introducing complexity when combined.
glossary.sentence: if it is not possible to avoid using OR, try avoiding the use of combinations of AND and OR in the same rule (Luger, 2009)

glossary.definition: The section of a production rule that contains actions or statements/conclusions.
glossary.sentence: try adding only one action or statement/conclusion to the THEN part of production rules (Luger, 2009)

glossary.definition: A conflict resolution strategy where the first or last found rule in the examination order is fired.
glossary.sentence: to fire the first/the last rule found. In this scheme, the conflict set is not assembled at all, and the order in which rules are selected for examination determines conflict resolution (Hopgood, 2001)

glossary.definition: An explicitly stated value associated with a rule to determine its firing order.
glossary.sentence: Rules can be written so that each has an explicitly stated priority value (Hopgood, 2001)

glossary.definition: A conflict resolution method where the most specific rule, or one with more conditions, is fired.
glossary.sentence: to fire more specific rules. This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one (Negnevitsky , 2011)

glossary.definition: Tags attached to each fact in working memory used to determine the order of firing rules.
glossary.sentence: A rule is more specific than another if it has more conditions: IF a THEN c IF a AND b THEN c. more specific rule fire the rule that uses the data most recently entered in the working memory. This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory; thus, in the conflict set, the expert system first fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011)

glossary.definition: The syntactic independence of rules allowing for incremental development of expert systems.
glossary.sentence: (the text fragment is based on the information given in (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011))

glossary.definition: The control cycle of expert systems where each stage corresponds to a recognition and action phase.
glossary.sentence: (the text fragment is based on the information given in (Luger, 2009))

glossary.definition: Values used to represent incomplete or uncertain knowledge in rule-based expert systems.
glossary.sentence: (Negnevitsky , 2011)

glossary.definition: Applications requiring immediate processing and response; rule-based expert systems with a large number of rules may be unsuitable due to computational resource consumption.
glossary.sentence: (Negnevitsky , 2011) Expert systems with a large set of rules (over 100 rules) can be slow, and thus can be unsuitable for real-time applications

glossary.definition: Rules that are no longer relevant or accurate in the context of rule-based expert systems' learning capability.
glossary.sentence: (Negnevitsky , 2011) rule-based expert systems are not able to learn from the experience and understand which rules are already out-of-date
